threats.title: Staking Pool Free-Riding and Share Theft via Unadjusted Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: The staking pools do not deduct user deposit balances when staking tokens into the rollup or challenge manager. This allows early depositors to withdraw their funds after the protocol move is made, forcing remaining depositors to bear 100% of the slashing risk, and enables stealing funds from new depositors if the pool is slashed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The accounting flaw breaks the fundamental conservation of liabilities in the pool. If a pool is slashed, its token balance drops to 0 but user deposit balances remain positive. Any subsequent deposits can be immediately drained by old depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositBalance[msg.sender] is only decreased during withdrawal, not when funds are sent to the protocol

preconditions: Pool is funded by multiple users

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdrawAmount <= depositBalance[msg.sender] without accounting for slashed or locked funds
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Users can withdraw their original deposit amount even if the pool's tokens are locked or slashed, taking liquidity from other users
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can participate in staking with zero risk by withdrawing immediately after the protocol move, or steal new deposits after a slash

economic_questions: Can an attacker consistently front-run the resolution to withdraw?
How much capital can be stolen from naive late depositors?
validation_requirements: Simulate a scenario where pool loses a challenge and a new user deposits
Verify that old users can withdraw the new user's tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Any amount to establish a deposit balance
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of new deposits made after a slash
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite ROI if risk is entirely shifted to others
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (deposit, wait for slash, wait for victim, withdraw)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Victim must deposit into the slashed pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a victim deposits into a slashed pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys trust in the staking pool mechanism

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Honest depositors in the staking pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss of deposited funds
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tokens deposited into the affected pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if pools are intended to be reused after a slash
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Validate depositBalance invariants during locked state

threats.title: First Edge Stake Permanently Locked on Challenge Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: When the first layer zero edge is created, its stake is held in the EdgeChallengeManager. If this edge loses the challenge, it is never confirmed, meaning `refundStake` cannot be called. Unlike rival edges whose stakes are sent to the `excessStakeReceiver`, the first edge's stake remains permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol intends for losing stakes to be used to reimburse honest challengers via the `excessStakeReceiver`. By locking the first edge's stake in the contract, the protocol fails to distribute these funds, breaking the economic incentive model.
preconditions: A layer zero edge is created first (hasRival == false)
A rival edge is subsequently created and wins the challenge

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Stake is routed to address(this) but only recoverable if status == Confirmed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious first assertions effectively burn their stake rather than transferring it to the DAO/honest parties
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but protocol loses expected revenue/reimbursement funds

economic_questions: Is there any alternative administrative mechanism to sweep locked stakes?
Does the lack of reimbursement break the honest validator incentive?
validation_requirements: Simulate a challenge where the first edge loses and verify its stake cannot be recovered by anyone

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of one layer zero edge stake

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time a malicious assertion is made first
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduces funds available for honest validator reimbursement

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of one full stake amount per failed first edge
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative sum of all failed first edge stakes

threats.title: Delay Buffer Replenishment Griefing via Integer Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: The delay buffer replenishment calculation uses integer division `(elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS`. A sequencer can intentionally post batches at intervals where this calculation truncates to zero, permanently preventing the buffer from recovering after a depletion.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the buffer cannot replenish, the force inclusion window remains permanently at its minimum threshold. This degrades the sequencer's ability to order transactions and makes the network highly susceptible to force inclusion attacks.
preconditions: Buffer is currently depleted below max

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sequencer posts batches every N blocks where N < 10000 / replenishRateInBasis
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can permanently degrade sequencer MEV protection or force inclusion delays

economic_questions: Is the gas cost of frequent batching economically viable for the sequencer?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact block interval required to force truncation to zero
Simulate batch posting at this interval and verify buffer does not replenish

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas costs for frequent L1 batch posting
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect MEV extraction via force inclusion manipulation

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a natural buffer depletion event
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degrades L2 UX and sequencer reliability

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: MEV value during shortened force inclusion windows

threats.title: Premature Force Inclusion via Missing Timestamp Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The `forceInclusion` function only verifies that the block-based delay has passed (`l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number`), but fails to verify the time-based delay (`delaySeconds`), despite the `ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon` error being defined in the codebase.
threats.investigation_rationale: On chains where block production can speed up, the block delay may expire significantly before the intended wall-clock time delay. Attackers can exploit this to force-include transactions earlier than the protocol intended.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon(); // Missing timestamp check

preconditions: Block production rate is faster than the assumed average used to set delayBlocks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only block number is checked, timestamp is ignored
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker monitors block speedups and force-includes transactions prematurely
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can bypass sequencer ordering to capture MEV or execute time-sensitive trades

economic_questions: How much variance exists in the host chain's block production rate?
What is the economic value of shaving minutes/hours off the force inclusion delay?
validation_requirements: Verify that delaySeconds is configured but ignored in forceInclusion

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI for specific MEV extractions

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During periods of fast block production on L1

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: MEV value of the force-included transactions

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Breaks Staking Pool Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: If the configured `stakeToken` is a fee-on-transfer token, `AbsBoldStakingPool` credits the user's `depositBalance` with the full `amount` while receiving less. This prevents the pool from ever reaching the required balance to stake, or allows early withdrawers to drain funds deposited by others.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation states it assumes standard ERC20 behavior, making this a config-dependent risk. If a DAO configures a non-standard token, the staking pool mechanism will completely break, trapping funds.
threats.config_evidence: Config.sol defines stakeToken as an arbitrary address; createERC20Rollup.ts allows custom fee tokens.
preconditions: stakeToken is configured as a fee-on-transfer token

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: depositBalance increases by amount, but actual balance increases by amount - fee
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User deposits X, pool gets X-fee, user withdraws X, draining fee from other users' deposits
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can drain the pool's token balance, leaving other users with unbacked depositBalances

economic_questions: Will the protocol ever be deployed with a fee-on-transfer token?
Can the pool ever successfully create an assertion if fees are taken?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock fee-on-transfer token and attempt to deposit and withdraw

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of fees paid by other users
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Low, but causes complete DoS for the pool

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Other users must have deposited into the pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete failure of the staking pool mechanism

threats.title: Blob Gas Cost Truncation Denies Batch Poster Reimbursement
threats.hypothesis_description: In `SequencerInbox.formBlobDataHash`, the normalized blob gas cost is calculated as `blobCost / block.basefee`. If the L1 base fee spikes significantly higher than the blob base fee, this integer division can truncate to zero, denying the batch poster any L2 gas reimbursement for their blob data costs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Batch posters rely on accurate spending reports to be reimbursed on L2 for the L1 gas they spend. Systematic truncation during high L1 congestion forces the batch poster to operate at a loss, potentially causing them to halt operations and degrade rollup liveness.
preconditions: Batch poster is using EIP-4844 blobs
L1 block.basefee is significantly larger than (blobBaseFee * GAS_PER_BLOB * dataHashes.length)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Integer division blobCost / block.basefee drops remainders
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural market conditions cause truncation, denying reimbursement
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but causes economic loss to the protocol's batch poster infrastructure

economic_questions: Under what historical gas conditions does this truncation occur?
What is the cumulative financial loss to the batch poster over time?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact ratio of basefee to blobBaseFee required for truncation to zero

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High L1 congestion, low blob congestion
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: May force batch posters to pause operations during spikes to avoid financial loss

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative blob posting costs during spikes
